en.extract:
The red-wattled lapwing (Vanellus indicus) is an Asian lapwing or large plover, a wader in the family Charadriidae. Like other lapwings they are ground birds that are incapable of perching. Their characteristic loud alarm calls are indicators of human or animal movements and the sounds have been variously rendered as did he do it or pity to do it leading to the colloquial name of did-he-do-it bird. Usually seen in pairs or small groups not far from water, they sometimes form large aggregations in the non-breeding season (winter). They nest in a ground scrape laying three to four camouflaged eggs. Adults near the nest fly around, diving at potential predators while calling noisily. The cryptically patterned chicks hatch and immediately follow their parents to feed, hiding by lying low on the ground or in the grass when threatened.


Taxonomy
Traditionally well known to native hunters, the red-wattled lapwing was first described in a book by the French polymath Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon in his Histoire Naturelle des Oiseaux in 1781. The bird was also illustrated in a hand-coloured plate engraved by François-Nicolas Martinet in the Planches Enluminées D'Histoire Naturelle produced under the supervision of Edme-Louis Daubenton to accompany Buffon's text. Neither the plate nor Buffon's description included a scientific name but in 1783 the Dutch naturalist Pieter Boddaert used the binomial name Tringa indica in his catalogue of the Planches Enluminées. The type locality is Goa in western India. It was subsequently placed in various other genera such as Sarcogrammus and Lobivanellus before being merged into Vanellus which was erected by the French zoologist Mathurin Jacques Brisson in 1760. Vanellus is the Medieval Latin for a "lapwing". It is a diminutive of the Latin vanus meaning "winnowing" or "fan". The specific epithet indicus is the Latin for "India".
Across their wide range there are slight differences in the plumage and there are four recognized subspecies:

V. i. aigneri (Laubmann, 1913) – southeast Turkey to Pakistan
V. i. indicus (Boddaert, 1783) – central Pakistan to Nepal, northeast India and Bangladesh
V. i. lankae (Koelz, 1939) – Sri Lanka
V. i. atronuchalis (Jerdon, 1864) – northeast India to south China, southeast Asia, Malay Peninsula and north Sumatra


Description
Red-wattled lapwings are large waders, about 35 cm (14 in) long. The wings and back are light brown with a purple to green sheen, but the head, a bib on the front and back of the neck are black.  Prominently white patch runs between these two colours, from belly and tail, flanking the neck to the sides of crown.  Short tail is tipped black.  A red fleshy wattle in front of each eye, black-tipped red bill, and the long legs are yellow. In flight, prominent white wing bars formed by the white on the secondary coverts.
Race aigneri is slightly paler and larger than the nominate race and is found in Turkey, Iran, Iraq, Afghanistan and the Indus valley. The nominate race is found all over India. The Sri Lankan race lankae is smaller and dark while atronuchalis the race in north-eastern India and eastern Bangladesh has a white cheek surrounded by black.
Males and females are similar in plumage but males have a 5% longer wing and tend to have a longer carpal spur. The length of the birds is 320–350 mm, wing of 208–247 mm with the nominate averaging 223 mm, Sri Lanka 217 mm. The Bill is 31–36 mm and tarsus of 70–83 mm. Tail length is 104–128 mm.
It usually keeps in pairs or trios in well-watered open country, ploughed fields, grazing land, and margins and dry beds of tanks and puddles. They occasionally form large flocks, ranging from 26 to 200 birds. It is also found in forest clearings around rain-filled depressions. It runs about in short spurts and dips forward obliquely (with unflexed legs) to pick up food in a typical plover manner. They are said to feed at night being especially active around the full moon. Is uncannily and ceaselessly vigilant, day or night, and is the first to detect intrusions and raise an alarm, and was therefore considered a nuisance by hunters. Flight rather slow, with deliberate flaps, but capable of remarkable agility when defending nest or being hunted by a hawk.
Its striking appearance is supplemented by its noisy nature, with a loud and scolding did-he-do-it call, uttered both in the day and night.
Leucistic abnormal plumages have been noted.
The local names are mainly onomatopoeic in origin and include titahri (Hindi), titawi (Marathi), tittibha (Kannada), tateehar (Sindhi), titodi (Gujarati), hatatut (Kashmiri), balighora (Assamese), yennappa chitawa (Telugu), aal-kaati (Tamil, meaning "human indicator").


Distribution
It breeds from West Asia (Iraq, SW Iran, Persian Gulf) eastwards across South Asia (Baluchistan, Sri Lanka, Afghanistan, Pakistan, the entire Indian subcontinent up to Kanyakumari and up to 1800m in Kashmir/Nepal), with another sub-species further east in Southeast Asia. May migrate altitudinally in spring and autumn (e.g. in N. Baluchistan or NW Pakistan), and spreads out widely in the monsoons on creation of requisite habitats, but by and large the populations are resident.
This species is declining in its western range, but is abundant in much of South Asia, being seen at almost any wetland habitat in its range.


Behaviour and ecology
The breeding season is mainly March to August. The courtship involves the male puffing its feathers and pointing its beak upwards. The male then shuffles around the female. Several males may display to females and they may be close together. The eggs are laid in a ground scrape or depression sometimes fringed with pebbles, goat or hare droppings. About 3–4 black-blotched buff eggs shaped a bit like a peg-top (pyriform), 42x30 mm on average. Nests are difficult to find since the eggs are cryptically coloured and usually matches the ground pattern. In residential areas, they sometimes take to nesting on roof-tops. They have been recorded nesting on the stones between the rails of a railway track, the adult leaving the nest when trains passed. Nests that have been threatened by agricultural operations have been manually translocated by gradually shifting the eggs. When nesting they will attempt to dive bomb or distract potential predators. Both the male and female incubate the eggs and divert predators using distraction displays or flash their wings to deter any herbivores that threaten the nest. Males appear to relieve females incubating at the nest particularly towards the hot part of noon. The eggs hatch in 28 to 30 days. The reproductive success is about 40%. Egg mortality is high (~43%) due to predation by mongooses, crows and kites. Chicks have a lower mortality (8.3%) and their survival improves after the first week.
Like other lapwings, they soak their belly feathers to provide water to their chicks as well as to cool the eggs during hot weather.

They bathe in pools of water when available and will often spend time on preening when leaving the nest or after copulation. They sometimes rest on the ground with the tarsi laid flat on the ground and at other times may rest on one leg.
Healthy adult birds have few predators and are capable of rapid and agile flight when pursued by hawks or falcons. Hugh B. Cott claimed that the flesh of the bird was unpalatable based on evidence from an Indian geologist who noted that a hungry tiger cub refused to eat their meat. Some endoparasitic tapeworms, nematodes, and trematodes have been described from the species. Mortality caused by respiratory infection by Ornithobacterium rhinotracheale has been recorded in captive birds in Pakistan.


Diet
The diet of the lapwing includes a range of insects, snails and other invertebrates, mostly picked from the ground. They may also feed on some grains. They feed mainly during the day but they may also feed at night. They may sometimes make use of the legs to disturb insect prey from soft soil.


In culture
In parts of India, a local belief is that the bird sleeps on its back with the legs upwards and an associated Hindi metaphor Titahri se asman thama jayega ("can the lapwing support the heavens?") is used to refer to persons undertaking tasks beyond their ability or strength.
In parts of Rajasthan it is believed that the laying of eggs by the lapwing on high ground was an indication of good rains to come. The eggs are known to be collected by practitioners of folk medicine. The Bhils of Malwa believed that the laying of eggs by red-wattled lapwings in the dry beds of streams as forewarnings of delayed rains or droughts. Eggs laid on the banks on the other hand were taken as indications of normal rains.


References


Other sources
Anon. (1991) Flocking of Red Wattled Lapwings. Newsletter for Birdwatchers 31(5–6):1.
Dharmakumarsinhji, RS (1965) Small displacement by ground nesting birds. Newsletter for Birdwatchers 5(9):10.
Gay, Thomas (1975). "More about the nesting of the Red-wattled Lapwing". Newsletter for Birdwatchers. 15 (4): 9.
Jamdar, Nitin (1985) Redwattled Lapwing (Vanellus indicus) suffering from cataract. Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society 82(1):197.
Kalsi, RS; Khera, S (1986) Some observations on breeding and displacement behaviour of the Redwattled Lapwing Vanellus indicus indicus (Aves: Charadriidae). Res. Bull. Panjab Univ. 37:131–141.
Khajuria, H (1972) Nestlings of the redwattled lapwing, Vanellus i. indicus (boddaert). Pavo 8(1&2):82–83.
Koshy, MS (1989) Lapwings on a roof. Newsletter for Birdwatchers 29(7–8):7.
Krishnan, M (1998) Ubiquitous alarmist. Blackbuck. 14(3&4):88–90.
Jackson, P (1976) Redwattled Lapwing. Newsletter for Birdwatchers 16(3):11–12.
Saxena, VS (1973) Unusual nesting by Redwattled Lapwing. Indian Forester 99:33–35.


External links

Photos of holotype for Vanellus indicus lankae Archived 10 August 2024 at the Wayback Machine

''Sarcogrammus indicus'' | range_map = Vanellus indicus map.svg | range_map_caption = Bounding distribution range }} The '''red-wattled lapwing''' ('''''Vanellus indicus''''') is an Asian [[lapwing]] or large [[plover]], a [[wader]] in the family [[Charadriidae]]. Like other lapwings they are ground birds that are incapable of perching. Their characteristic loud alarm calls are indicators of human or animal movements and the sounds have been variously rendered as ''did he do it'' or ''pity to do it''{{cite book|author=Jerdon, TC|year=1864|pages=[https://archive.org/details/birdsofindiabein03jerd/page/648 648]–649|title=The Birds of India|publisher=George Wyman & Co|url=https://archive.org/details/birdsofindiabein03jerd}} leading to the colloquial name of ''did-he-do-it'' bird.{{cite journal|author=Symons, CT|year=1917|title= Note on the breeding habits of the Did-he-do-it ''Sarcogrammus indicus''|journal=Spolia Zeylanica|volume=10|issue=39|pages=397–398|url=https://archive.org/stream/spoliazeylanica10nati#page/398/mode/1up/}} Usually seen in pairs or small groups not far from water, they sometimes form large aggregations in the non-breeding season (winter). They nest in a ground scrape laying three to four camouflaged eggs. Adults near the nest fly around, diving at potential predators while calling noisily. The cryptically patterned chicks hatch and immediately follow their parents to feed, hiding by lying low on the ground or in the grass when threatened. ==Taxonomy== Traditionally well known to native hunters, the red-wattled lapwing was first described in a book by the French polymath [[Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon]] in his ''Histoire Naturelle des Oiseaux'' in 1781.{{cite book | last=Buffon | first=Georges-Louis Leclerc de | author-link=Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon | year=1781 | title=Histoire Naturelle des Oiseaux | volume=15 | place=Paris | publisher=De L'Imprimerie Royale | page=101 | chapter=Le Vanneau armé des Indes | language=fr | chapter-url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/42298494 }} The bird was also illustrated in a hand-coloured plate engraved by [[François-Nicolas Martinet]] in the ''Planches Enluminées D'Histoire Naturelle'' produced under the supervision of [[Edme-Louis Daubenton]] to accompany Buffon's text.{{cite book | last1=Buffon | first1=Georges-Louis Leclerc de | author1-link=Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon | last2=Martinet | first2=François-Nicolas | author2-link=François-Nicolas Martinet | last3=Daubenton | first3=Edme-Louis | author3-link=Edme-Louis Daubenton | last4=Daubenton | first4=Louis-Jean-Marie | author4-link=Louis-Jean-Marie Daubenton | year=1765–1783 | chapter=Vanneau armé, de Goa | title=Planches Enluminées D'Histoire Naturelle | volume=9 | place=Paris | publisher=De L'Imprimerie Royale | at=Plate 807 | chapter-url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35224517 }} Neither the plate nor Buffon's description included a scientific name but in 1783 the Dutch naturalist [[Pieter Boddaert]] used the [[binomial name]] ''Tringa indica'' in his catalogue of the ''Planches Enluminées''.{{cite book | last=Boddaert | first=Pieter | author-link=Pieter Boddaert | year=1783 | title=Table des planches enluminéez d'histoire naturelle de M. D'Aubenton: avec les denominations de M.M. de Buffon, Brisson, Edwards, Linnaeus et Latham, precedé d'une notice des principaux ouvrages zoologiques enluminés | place=Utrecht | page=50, Number 807 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/27822670 | language=fr }} The [[Type locality (biology)|type locality]] is [[Goa]] in western India.{{cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1934 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=2 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=238 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14483051 }} It was subsequently placed in various other [[genus|genera]] such as ''Sarcogrammus'' and ''Lobivanellus'' before being merged into ''[[Vanellus]]'' which was erected by the French zoologist [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson]] in 1760.{{cite book | last=Brisson | first=Mathurin Jacques | author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson | year=1760 | title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode contenant la division des oiseaux en ordres, sections, genres, especes & leurs variétés | volume=1 | language=fr, la | page=48 | place=Paris | publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36010447 }}{{cite book | last=Brisson | first=Mathurin Jacques | author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson | year=1760 | title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode contenant la division des oiseaux en ordres, sections, genres, especes & leurs variétés | volume=5 | language=fr, la | page=94 | place=Paris | publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36294364 }} ''Vanellus'' is the [[Medieval Latin]] for a "lapwing". It is a diminutive of the Latin ''vanus'' meaning "winnowing" or "fan". The specific epithet ''indicus'' is the Latin for "India".{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn= 978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages=[https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n204 204], 398 }} Across their wide range there are slight differences in the plumage and there are four recognized [[subspecies]]:{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2019 | title=Grebes, flamingos, buttonquail, plovers, painted-snipes, jacanas, plains-wanderer, seedsnipes | work=World Bird List Version 9.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/grebes/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=26 June 2019 }} * ''V. i. aigneri'' ([[Alfred Laubmann|Laubmann]], 1913) – southeast Turkey to Pakistan * ''V. i. indicus'' (Boddaert, 1783) – central Pakistan to Nepal, [[northeast India]] and Bangladesh * ''V. i. lankae'' ([[Walter Koelz|Koelz]], 1939) – Sri Lanka * ''V. i. atronuchalis'' ([[Thomas C. Jerdon|Jerdon]], 1864) – northeast India to south China, southeast Asia, Malay Peninsula and north Sumatra Red-wattled lapwing-wings-spread-tail-hyderabad-India.jpg|''V. i. indicus''
showing the diagnostic white wing bar and a broad black band on the white tail Vanellus indicus.jpg|''V. i. aigneri''
[[Turkey]] Vanellus indicus - Laem Phak Bia.jpg|''V. i. atronuchalis'' showing the white ear patch bounded by black, [[Thailand]] Red-wattled lapwing (Vanellus indicus lankae).jpg|''V. i. lankae''
[[Sri Lanka]] ==Description== Red-wattled lapwings are large [[wader]]s, about {{convert|35|cm|in|abbr=on}} long. The wings and back are light brown with a purple to green sheen, but the head, a bib on the front and back of the neck are black. Prominently white patch runs between these two colours, from belly and tail, flanking the neck to the sides of crown. Short tail is tipped black. A red fleshy [[wattle (anatomy)|wattle]] in front of each eye, black-tipped red bill, and the long legs are yellow. In flight, prominent white wing bars formed by the white on the secondary coverts.{{cite book|author1=Ali, S |author2=S D Ripley |name-list-style=amp | title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan|edition=2nd|publisher=Oxford University Press|volume= 2|year= 1980|pages =212–215 }} Race ''aigneri'' is slightly paler and larger than the nominate race and is found in Turkey, Iran, Iraq, Afghanistan and the Indus valley. The nominate race is found all over India. The Sri Lankan race ''lankae'' is smaller and dark while ''atronuchalis'' the race in north-eastern India and eastern Bangladesh has a white cheek surrounded by black.{{cite book|year=2005|title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide|publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions|isbn=84-87334-67-9|author1=Pamela C. Rasmussen |author2=John C. Anderton |name-list-style=amp |oclc=60359701}} Males and females are similar in plumage but males have a 5% longer wing and tend to have a longer carpal spur. The length of the birds is 320–350 mm, wing of 208–247 mm with the nominate averaging 223 mm, Sri Lanka 217 mm. The Bill is 31–36 mm and tarsus of 70–83 mm. Tail length is 104–128 mm. It usually keeps in pairs or trios in well-watered open country, ploughed fields, grazing land, and margins and dry beds of tanks and puddles. They occasionally form large flocks, ranging from 26 to 200 birds.{{cite journal|author=Vyas, Rakesh|year=1997|title=Flocking and courtship display in Redwattled Lapwing (''Vanellus indicus'')|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=94|pages= 406–407|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48601768}} It is also found in forest clearings around rain-filled depressions. It runs about in short spurts and dips forward obliquely (with unflexed legs) to pick up food in a typical plover manner. {{cite book|last = Ali|first = Salim|author-link=Salim Ali (ornithologist)|title = Book of Indian Birds, Salim Ali centenary edition |year = 1996|publisher=[[Bombay Natural History Society]]/[[Oxford University Press]]|location = Mumbai|page=139 }} They are said to feed at night being especially active around the full moon.{{cite book|author=Hayman, P.|author2=J. Marchant|author3=T. Prater|year=1986|title=Shorebirds: an identification guide to the waders of the world|publisher=Croom Helm, London|pages=274–275}} Is uncannily and ceaselessly vigilant, day or night, and is the first to detect intrusions and raise an alarm, and was therefore considered a nuisance by hunters. Flight rather slow, with deliberate flaps, but capable of remarkable agility when defending nest or being hunted by a hawk. Its striking appearance is supplemented by its noisy nature, with a loud and scolding ''did-he-do-it'' call, uttered both in the day and night. [[Leucism|Leucistic]] abnormal [[plumage]]s have been noted.{{cite journal|author1=Mehra SP|author2=N Singh |author3=S Mehra |name-list-style=amp|year=2008| title= Sighting of a partially albino Red-wattled Lapwing ''Vanellus indicus'' in Udaipur, Rajasthan| journal=Indian Birds |volume=4| issue=3|page=120}} The local names are mainly onomatopoeic in origin and include {{transliteration|hi|titahri}} (Hindi), {{transliteration|mr|titawi}} (Marathi), {{transliteration|kn|tittibha}} (Kannada), {{transliteration|sd|tateehar}} (Sindhi), {{transliteration|gu|titodi}} (Gujarati), {{transliteration|ks|hatatut}} (Kashmiri), {{transliteration|as|balighora}} (Assamese), {{transliteration|te|yennappa chitawa}} (Telugu), {{transliteration|ta|aal-kaati}} (Tamil, meaning "human indicator"). ==Distribution== It breeds from West Asia ([[Iraq]], SW [[Iran]], Persian Gulf) eastwards across South Asia ([[Balochistan (region)|Baluchistan]], [[Sri Lanka]], Afghanistan, [[Pakistan]], the entire [[India]]n subcontinent up to [[Kanyakumari district|Kanyakumari]] and up to 1800m in [[Kashmir]]/[[Nepal]]), with another sub-species further east in Southeast Asia. May [[bird migration|migrate]] altitudinally in spring and autumn (e.g. in N. [[Balochistan (region)|Baluchistan]] or NW [[Pakistan]]), and spreads out widely in the monsoons on creation of requisite habitats, but by and large the populations are resident.{{cite journal|author=Saini, SS |year=1972| title= Unexpected summer visitors in the Himalayas – Redwattled Lapwing|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW12#page/n100/mode/1up|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=12|issue=8|pages=5–6}} This species is declining in its western range, but is abundant in much of South Asia, being seen at almost any wetland habitat in its range. ==Behaviour and ecology== The breeding season is mainly March to August. The courtship involves the male puffing its feathers and pointing its beak upwards. The male then shuffles around the female. Several males may display to females and they may be close together. The eggs are laid in a ground scrape or depression sometimes fringed with pebbles, goat or hare droppings.{{cite journal|author=Sharma, SK|year=1992|title=Use of droppings of Indian Hare for nest making by Redwattled Lapwing|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=32|issue=7&8|page=19|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW32_78#page/n19/mode/1up}} About 3–4 black-blotched buff eggs shaped a bit like a peg-top ([[wikt:pyriform|pyriform]]), 42x30 mm on average. Nests are difficult to find since the eggs are cryptically coloured and usually matches the ground pattern. In residential areas, they sometimes take to nesting on roof-tops.{{cite journal|author=Mundkur, Taej|year=1985|title=Observations on the roof-nesting habit of the Redwattled Lapwing (''Vanellus indicus'') in Poona, Maharashtra|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=82|issue=1|pages=194–196|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/50394858}}{{cite journal|author1=Tehsin, Raza H |author2=Lokhandwala, Juzer |year=1982|title=Unusual nesting of Redwattled Lapwing (''Vanellus indicus'')|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=79|issue=2|page=414|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48744830}}{{cite journal|author=Reeves, SK |year=1975 |title= Unusual nesting by Red-wattled Lapwing|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=15| issue=2| pages=5–6| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW15#page/n16/mode/1up/}} They have been recorded nesting on the stones between the rails of a railway track, the adult leaving the nest when trains passed.{{cite journal|last=McCann|first=Charles|year=1941|title= Curious nesting site of the Red-wattled Lapwing (''Lobivanellus indicus indicus'' Bodd.)|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=42|issue=2|pages=441–442|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47873210}} Nests that have been threatened by agricultural operations have been manually translocated by gradually shifting the eggs.{{cite journal|author1=Sridhar, S |author2=Karanth, P |year=1991| title= Dilemma near the nest of a pair of red-wattled lapwings|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=31|issue=7&8| pages=7–9| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW31_78#page/n8/mode/1up/}} When nesting they will attempt to dive bomb or distract potential predators.{{cite journal|author=Rangaswami, S|year=1980|title=Lapwing fighting off cobra|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=20|issue=1|page=13|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW20#page/n13/mode/1up}}{{cite journal|author=Bhatnagar, RK|year=1978|title=Interaction of a Redwattled Lapwing and a dog|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=18|issue=1|page=9|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW18_1#page/n9/mode/1up}}{{cite journal|author=Bhagwat, VR|year=1991|title=Lapwings and snake|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=31|issue=5&6|pages=10–11|url= https://archive.org/stream/NLBW31_56#page/n11/mode/1up/ }}{{cite journal|author1=Kalsi, RS |author2=Khera, S |year=1987|title=Agonistic and distraction behaviour of the Redwattled Lapwing, ''Vanellus indicus indicus''|journal=Pavo|volume=25|issue=1&2|pages=43–56}} Both the male and female incubate the eggs and divert predators using [[distraction display]]s or flash their wings to deter any [[Herbivore|herbivores]] that threaten the nest. Males appear to relieve females incubating at the nest particularly towards the hot part of noon.{{cite journal|author1=Naik, RM |author2=George, PV |author3=Dixit, Dhruv B |year=1961|title=Some observations on the behaviour of the incubating Redwattled Lapwing, ''Vanellus indicus indicus'' (Bodd.) |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=58|issue=1|pages=223–230|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47540769}} The eggs hatch in 28 to 30 days. The reproductive success is about 40%. Egg mortality is high (~43%) due to predation by mongooses, crows and kites. Chicks have a lower mortality (8.3%) and their survival improves after the first week.{{cite journal|author1=Desai, JH |author2=Malhotra, AK |year=1976|title=A note on incubation period and reproductive success of the Redwattled Lapwing, ''Vanellus indicus'' at Delhi Zoological Park |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=73|issue=2|pages=392–394|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48293395}} Like other lapwings, they soak their belly feathers to provide water to their chicks as well as to cool the eggs during hot weather.{{cite journal|author=Sundararaman, V.|year=1989|title=Belly-soaking and nest wetting behaviour of Redwattled Lapwing, ''Vanellus indicus'' (Boddaert)|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=86|page=242|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48710614}}{{cite journal |author1=Kalsi, R. S. |author2=S. Khera |name-list-style=amp |year=1990|title= Growth and development of the Red-wattled Lapwing ''Vanellus indicus''|journal=Stilt |volume=17|pages=57–64|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/216750532 }} File:Vanellus indicus aigneri MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.147.1.jpg|''V. i. aigneri'' eggs from [[MHNT]] File:Vanellus indicus eggs and chicks.jpg|Chicks and eggs on a scrape nest. The young hatch in synchrony and the cryptically plumaged chick typically lies still when alarmed. File:Red wattled lapwing immature.jpg|The chick leaves the nest and follows the parents soon after hatching File:Red-wattled lapwing (juvenile).jpg|A juvenile They bathe in pools of water when available and will often spend time on preening when leaving the nest or after copulation. They sometimes rest on the ground with the tarsi laid flat on the ground and at other times may rest on one leg.{{cite journal|author1=Kalsi, RS |author2=Khera, S |year=1992|title= Some observations on maintenance behaviour of the Red-wattled Lapwing ''Vanellus indicus'' (Boddaert)|journal=[[Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society]] |volume=89|issue=3|pages=368–372|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48732848}} Healthy adult birds have few predators and are capable of rapid and agile flight when pursued by hawks or falcons. [[Hugh B. Cott]] claimed that the flesh of the bird was unpalatable based on evidence from an Indian geologist who noted that a hungry tiger cub refused to eat their meat.{{Cite journal|last=Cott|first=Hugh B.|date=1946|title=The Edibility of Birds: Illustrated by Five Years' Experiments and Observations (1941–1946) on the Food Preferences of the Hornet, Cat and Man;and considered with Special Reference to the Theories of Adaptive Coloration|journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London|language=en|volume=116|issue=3–4|pages=371–524|doi=10.1111/j.1096-3642.1947.tb00131.x}} Some endoparasitic tapeworms, nematodes, and trematodes have been described from the species.{{cite journal|title=Two new tapeworm ''Panuwa ahilyai'' n. sp. and ''Panuwa shindei'' n. sp. from ''Vanellus indicus'' at Aurangabad, M.S., India |journal=[[Rivista di Parassitologia]]|author1=Jadhav. V. |author2=Nanware S. S. |author3=Rao S. S. |year=1994|volume=55|issue=3|pages=379–384}}{{cite journal|doi=10.1017/S0022149X00033046|title=On Some Spirurid and Filariid Nematodes of Birds in Pakistan|journal=Journal of Helminthology|volume=30|issue=2–3|pages=103–112|year=1956|last1=Sarwar|first1=M. M.|pmid=13346051|s2cid=37208401 }}{{cite journal|journal=Zeitschrift für Parasitenkunde |year=1962|volume=21|pages=212–4|title=''Uvitellina indica'' n. sp. (Trematoda: Cyclocoeliidae) from a redwattled lapwing, ''Lobivanellus indicus'' (Boddaert)|author=Siddiqi, AH|author2=Jairajpuri MS|issue=3|doi=10.1007/bf00260233|pmid=13912529|s2cid=9487751}} Mortality caused by respiratory infection by ''[[Ornithobacterium rhinotracheale]]'' has been recorded in captive birds in Pakistan.{{cite journal|author1=Umar, S.|author2=M. Iqbal|author3=A. H. Khan|author4=A. Mushtaq|author5=K. Aqil|author6=T. Jamil|author7=S. Asif|author8=N. Qamar|author9=A. Shahzad|year=2017|title=Ornithobacterium rhinotracheale infection in red wattled lapwings (Vanellus indicus) in Pakistan – a case report.|url=https://hrcak.srce.hr/file/274682|journal=Vet. Arhiv|volume=87|issue=5|pages=641–648|author10=M. Younus|doi=10.24099/vet.arhiv.160519b|doi-access=free}} ===Diet=== The diet of the lapwing includes a range of insects, [[snail]]s and other invertebrates, mostly picked from the ground. They may also feed on some grains. They feed mainly during the day but they may also feed at night. They may sometimes make use of the legs to disturb insect prey from soft soil.{{cite journal|author=Babi, AZ |year=1987| title= Feeding behaviour of red-wattled lapwing|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=27|issue=1–2|page=15| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW27#page/n15/mode/1up}} ==In culture== In parts of India, a local belief is that the bird sleeps on its back with the legs upwards and an associated Hindi metaphor ''Titahri se asman thama jayega'' ("can the lapwing support the heavens?") is used to refer to persons undertaking tasks beyond their ability or strength. In parts of Rajasthan it is believed that the laying of eggs by the lapwing on high ground was an indication of good rains to come.{{cite journal|author=Saxena VS|year=1974|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW14#page/n73/mode/1up|title=Unusual nesting by Red-wattled Lapwing|pages=3–5|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=14|issue=11}} The eggs are known to be collected by practitioners of [[Traditional medicine|folk medicine]].{{cite journal|journal=Our Nature|doi=10.3126/on.v1i1.303 |year=2003|volume=1|pages=37–41|title=An Ethnobiological Study of the Tamang People|author=Tamang, Ganesh |doi-access=free}}{{cite journal|author1=Negi, Chandra S. Negi|author2=Veerendra S. Palyal|name-list-style=amp|title=Traditional Uses of Animal and Animal Products in Medicine and Rituals by the Shauka Tribes of District Pithoragarh, Uttaranchal, India|journal=Studies on Ethno-Medicine|volume=1|issue=1|pages=47–54|year=2007|url=http://www.krepublishers.com/02-Journals/S-EM/EM-01-1-000-000-2007-Web/EM-01-1-000-000-2007Abst-PDF/EM-01-1-047-054-2007-006-Negi-C-S/EM-01-1-047-054-006-Negi-C-S-Tt.pdf|doi=10.1080/09735070.2007.11886300|s2cid=30993906|archive-date=30 April 2012|access-date=24 September 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120430132052/http://www.krepublishers.com/02-Journals/S-EM/EM-01-1-000-000-2007-Web/EM-01-1-000-000-2007Abst-PDF/EM-01-1-047-054-2007-006-Negi-C-S/EM-01-1-047-054-006-Negi-C-S-Tt.pdf}}{{cite journal|author1=Srinivas, K.V. |author2=S. Subramanya |name-list-style=amp |year=2000|title=Stealing of Redwattled Lapwing ''Vanellus indicus'' (Boddaert) and Yellow-wattled Lapwing ''Vanellus malabaricus'' (Boddaert) eggs by cowherds|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=97|issue=1|pages= 143–144|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48567569}} The Bhils of Malwa believed that the laying of eggs by red-wattled lapwings in the dry beds of streams as forewarnings of delayed rains or droughts. Eggs laid on the banks on the other hand were taken as indications of normal rains.{{cite book|author=Luard, C.E.|title=Jungle tribes of Malwa. The Ethnographical Survey of the Central India Agency. Monograph No. 11.|year=1909| place=Lucknow |url=https://archive.org/details/malwatribes/page/n26/mode/1up/ |page=27}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Other sources== * Anon. (1991) Flocking of Red Wattled Lapwings. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' 31(5–6):1. * [[Raol Shri Dharmakumarsinhji|Dharmakumarsinhji, RS]] (1965) Small displacement by ground nesting birds. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' 5(9):10. * {{cite journal| author=Gay, Thomas |year=1975| title= More about the nesting of the Red-wattled Lapwing|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=15|issue=4|page=9|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW15#page/n43/mode/1up/}} * Jamdar, Nitin (1985) [https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/50394861 Redwattled Lapwing (''Vanellus indicus'') suffering from cataract.] Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society 82(1):197. * Kalsi, RS; Khera, S (1986) Some observations on breeding and displacement behaviour of the Redwattled Lapwing ''Vanellus indicus indicus'' (Aves: Charadriidae). Res. Bull. Panjab Univ. 37:131–141. * Khajuria, H (1972) Nestlings of the redwattled lapwing, ''Vanellus i. indicus'' (boddaert). Pavo 8(1&2):82–83. * Koshy, MS (1989) Lapwings on a roof. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' 29(7–8):7. * [[Madhaviah Krishnan|Krishnan, M]] (1998) Ubiquitous alarmist. [[Blackbuck]]. 14(3&4):88–90. * Jackson, P (1976) Redwattled Lapwing. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' 16(3):11–12. * Saxena, VS (1973) Unusual nesting by Redwattled Lapwing. Indian Forester 99:33–35. ==External links== {{Commons category|Vanellus indicus}} {{Wikispecies|Vanellus indicus}} * [http://webapps.lsa.umich.edu/ummz/birds/collections/orderResult.asp?cOrder=Charadriiformes&offset=-1 Photos of holotype for ''Vanellus indicus lankae''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240810094116/https://webapps.lsa.umich.edu/ummz/birds/collections/orderResult.asp?cOrder=Charadriiformes&offset=-1 |date=10 August 2024 }} {{Taxonbar|from=Q752398}} [[Category:Vanellus|red-wattled lapwing]] [[Category:Birds of West Asia]] [[Category:Birds of South Asia]] [[Category:Birds of Indochina]] [[Category:Birds described in 1783|red-wattled lapwing]] [[Category:Taxa named by Pieter Boddaert|red-wattled lapwing]]

zh.extract:
肉垂麦鸡（学名：Vanellus indicus）为鸻科麦鸡属的鸟类。该物种的模式产地在印度。


亚种
肉垂麦鸡云南亚种（学名：Vanellus indicus atronuchalis）。在中国大陆，分布于云南等地。该物种的模式产地在缅甸。


参考文献

Anon. (1991) Flocking of Red Wattled Lapwings. Newsl. for Birdwatchers 31(5-6):1.
Dharmakumarsinhji,RS (1965)  Small displacement by ground nesting birds. Newsl. for Birdwatchers 5(9):10.
Gay,Thomas. More about the nesting of the Red-wattled Lapwing. Newsl. for Birdwatchers. 1975, 15 (4): 9. 
Jamdar,Nitin (1985) Redwattled Lapwing (Vanellus indicus) suffering from cataract. J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.  82(1):197.
Kalsi,RS; Khera,S (1986)  Some observations on breeding and displacement behaviour of the Redwattled Lapwing Vanellus indicus indicus (Aves: Charadriidae). Res. Bull. Panjab Univ. 37:131-141.
Khajuria,H (1972) Nestlings of the redwattled lapwing, Vanellus i. indicus (boddaert). Pavo 8(1&2):82-83.
Koshy,MS (1989) Lapwings on a roof. Newsl. for Birdwatchers 29(7-8):7.
Krishnan, M (1998) Ubiquitous alarmist. Blackbuck. 14(3&4):88-90.
Jackson,P (1976)  Redwattled Lapwing. Newsl. for Birdwatchers 16(3):11-12.
Saxena,VS (1973)  Unusual nesting by Redwattled Lapwing. Indian Forester 99:33-35.
